intent: Check whether the candidate understands TypeScript decorators as runtime functions that attach or transform metadata, and can explain how NestJS builds modules, routing, dependency injection, and request pipelines from that metadata.
primaryQuestion: What is a TypeScript decorator and how does NestJS use them?
expectedSignals: Defines a decorator as an expression evaluated to a function and applied to a class, method, property, accessor, or parameter.
Explains that decorators usually attach metadata or wrap/alter behavior rather than being normal runtime calls inside the handler body.
Connects NestJS decorators such as @Module, @Controller, @Injectable, @Get, @Body, and @UseGuards to metadata scanned by the framework.
Mentions that Nest uses metadata to build the DI container, route map, parameter extraction, validation pipeline, guards, interceptors, and Swagger metadata where applicable.
redFlags: Describes decorators as comments, annotations only, or compile-time-only TypeScript types.
Cannot distinguish decorator metadata from business logic inside the method.
Thinks NestJS decorators execute the HTTP request themselves instead of configuring framework behavior.
Ignores runtime requirements such as experimental decorators and metadata reflection in Nest-style TypeScript projects.

scoringHints.description: Explains decorator mechanics, valid targets, metadata attachment, and concrete Nest examples across routing, DI, and request pipeline configuration.
Understands decorators as metadata-driven framework configuration but misses one detail such as decorator targets or DI metadata.
Can name common decorators but cannot explain what they do under the hood.
Answer is generic or treats decorators as syntax sugar without explaining runtime metadata.

followUps.question: Pick @Controller('users') and @Get(':id'). What metadata do they give Nest, and when does Nest use it?
Which NestJS features would become harder if the framework could not read decorator metadata?
followUps.purpose: Check understanding of the decorator-driven framework model.

followUps.question: Are decorators the same thing as TypeScript interfaces? Why or why not?
followUps.purpose: Separate runtime metadata from erased compile-time types.

en.title: TypeScript decorators in NestJS
en.displayQuestion: What is a TypeScript decorator, and how does NestJS use decorators?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands decorators as runtime metadata and framework wiring.
guidance: Explain decorator targets: class, method, property, accessor, and parameter.
Use Nest examples such as @Module, @Controller, @Injectable, @Get, @Body, and @UseGuards.
Connect the syntax to Nest scanning metadata for DI, routing, and request lifecycle behavior.
commonMistakes: Calling decorators comments or compile-time-only annotations.
Listing decorators without explaining metadata or framework scanning.
Confusing TypeScript type declarations with runtime metadata.

vi.title: Decorator TypeScript trong NestJS
vi.displayQuestion: TypeScript decorator là gì, và NestJS sử dụng decorator như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có hiểu decorator là cơ chế runtime metadata để Nest wiring framework hay không.
guidance: Giải thích decorator có thể gắn lên class, method, property, accessor và parameter.
Dùng ví dụ Nest như @Module, @Controller, @Injectable, @Get, @Body và @UseGuards.
Kết nối cú pháp decorator với việc Nest scan metadata để dựng DI, routing và request lifecycle.
commonMistakes: Nói decorator chỉ là comment hoặc annotation chỉ tồn tại lúc compile.
Chỉ liệt kê decorator mà không giải thích metadata và cơ chế scan của framework.
Nhầm TypeScript type/interface với runtime metadata.

ja.title: NestJS における TypeScript decorator
ja.displayQuestion: TypeScript decorator とは何ですか。NestJS は decorator をどのように使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: decorator を runtime metadata と framework wiring の仕組みとして理解しているか確認する。
guidance: class、method、property、accessor、parameter に適用できることを説明する。
@Module、@Controller、@Injectable、@Get、@Body、@UseGuards などの Nest 例を使う。
Nest が metadata を読み取り、DI、routing、request lifecycle を構成する流れにつなげる。
commonMistakes: decorator を comment や compile-time-only annotation として説明する。
metadata や framework scanning を説明せず decorator 名だけを列挙する。
TypeScript の type/interface と runtime metadata を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: TypeScript Handbook: Decorators
sourceReferences.note: Used for TypeScript decorator concepts and decorator targets.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers
sourceReferences.note: Used for Nest controller and route decorator behavior.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Modules
sourceReferences.note: Used for module decorator metadata framing.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands reflect-metadata, TypeScript emitted design metadata, and why NestJS depends on runtime metadata for constructor injection and decorator-driven framework behavior.
primaryQuestion: What does reflect-metadata do and why is it required?
expectedSignals: Explains that reflect-metadata provides APIs such as Reflect.defineMetadata and Reflect.getMetadata for storing and reading metadata at runtime.
Mentions TypeScript compiler options such as experimentalDecorators and emitDecoratorMetadata in Nest-style projects.
Connects emitted design metadata such as design:paramtypes to Nest dependency injection and constructor parameter discovery.
Clarifies limits: interfaces, generics, unions, and many erased TypeScript types are not fully available at runtime.
redFlags: Says reflect-metadata performs validation or type checking by itself.
Thinks TypeScript interfaces are available to Nest at runtime.
Cannot explain why removing reflect-metadata or decorator metadata can break dependency injection.
Treats reflection metadata as database metadata or request metadata.

scoringHints.description: Connects reflect-metadata APIs, emitted design:paramtypes metadata, Nest DI, decorator metadata reading, and runtime type limitations.
Understands that Nest reads runtime metadata but misses exact metadata keys or TypeScript erasure limitations.
Knows reflect-metadata by name but cannot explain why Nest needs it.
Answer is inaccurate or confuses reflection metadata with unrelated concepts.

followUps.question: How can Nest know that constructor(private usersService: UsersService) needs UsersService?
If a constructor parameter is typed as an interface, can Nest inject it automatically from type metadata? What would you do instead?
followUps.purpose: Test runtime type erasure and custom injection token knowledge.

followUps.question: What kinds of TypeScript type information are not reliable at runtime even with emitDecoratorMetadata enabled?

en.title: reflect-metadata and NestJS runtime metadata
en.displayQuestion: What does reflect-metadata do, and why is it required in NestJS-style TypeScript projects?
en.displayIntent: Check runtime metadata knowledge behind Nest dependency injection and decorators.
guidance: Mention Reflect metadata APIs and TypeScript emitted design metadata.
Explain how Nest uses constructor parameter metadata for dependency injection.
Call out limits around interfaces, generics, unions, and erased types.
commonMistakes: Saying reflect-metadata validates request DTOs by itself.
Assuming TypeScript interfaces exist at runtime.
Ignoring the relation between metadata and Nest's IoC container.

vi.title: reflect-metadata và runtime metadata trong NestJS
vi.displayQuestion: reflect-metadata làm gì, và vì sao nó cần thiết trong project TypeScript kiểu NestJS?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về runtime metadata phía sau dependency injection và decorator của Nest.
guidance: Nhắc tới Reflect metadata API và design metadata do TypeScript emit ra.
Giải thích Nest dùng metadata của constructor parameter để resolve dependency injection.
Nêu giới hạn với interface, generic, union và các type đã bị erase.
commonMistakes: Nói reflect-metadata tự validate DTO hoặc request.
Cho rằng TypeScript interface tồn tại ở runtime.
Bỏ qua mối liên hệ giữa metadata và IoC container của Nest.

ja.title: reflect-metadata と NestJS の runtime metadata
ja.displayQuestion: reflect-metadata は何をし、NestJS 型の TypeScript project でなぜ必要ですか。
ja.displayIntent: Nest の dependency injection と decorator の背後にある runtime metadata を理解しているか確認する。
guidance: Reflect metadata API と TypeScript が emit する design metadata に触れる。
Nest が constructor parameter metadata を使って dependency injection を解決する流れを説明する。
interface、generic、union、erased type の限界を述べる。
commonMistakes: reflect-metadata が request DTO を自動 validation すると説明する。
TypeScript interface が runtime に存在すると考える。
metadata と Nest の IoC container の関係を説明しない。

sourceReferences.label: TypeScript TSConfig: emitDecoratorMetadata
sourceReferences.note: Used for TypeScript design metadata behavior.

sourceReferences.label: TypeScript Handbook: Decorator metadata
sourceReferences.note: Used for reflect-metadata and emitted type metadata concepts.

sourceReferences.label: reflect-metadata package
sourceReferences.note: Used for Metadata Reflection API package behavior.

intent: Check whether the candidate can distinguish NestJS HTTP parameter decorators and choose the correct source for request body, route parameters, query string, and headers.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between @Body(), @Param(), @Query(), and @Headers()?
expectedSignals: Explains @Body() reads req.body, typically used for POST, PUT, PATCH payloads and DTOs.
Explains @Param() reads path parameters from route patterns such as @Get(':id').
Explains @Query() reads query string values such as ?page=1&limit=20.
Explains @Headers() reads request headers and can extract a specific header by name.
Mentions that each decorator can read the whole object or a single key, for example @Param('id') or @Body('email').
redFlags: Uses @Param() for query strings or @Query() for path segments.
Thinks @Headers() sets response headers instead of reading request headers.
Cannot give URL examples showing path params versus query params.
Treats all incoming data as @Body() regardless of HTTP semantics.

scoringHints.description: Clearly maps all four decorators to request sources and gives concrete route examples with single-key extraction.
Gets the main mapping correct but misses a nuance such as whole-object versus key extraction.
Confuses at least two request sources or cannot map decorators to Express/Fastify request data.
Answer remains generic and does not show practical Nest controller knowledge.

followUps.question: For GET /users/42?expand=profile with an Authorization header, which decorator reads 42, expand, and the header?
Would @Param('id') read ?id=42? Why or why not?
followUps.purpose: Detect route parameter versus query parameter confusion.

followUps.question: When would you type @Body() as a DTO class instead of accepting any?
followUps.purpose: Connect parameter extraction to validation and API contract design.

en.title: HTTP parameter decorators
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between @Body(), @Param(), @Query(), and @Headers()?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can map Nest parameter decorators to the correct HTTP request source.
guidance: Use one URL example with path params, query params, body, and headers.
Mention whole-object extraction and single-key extraction.
Connect @Body() with DTOs and validation where appropriate.
commonMistakes: Using @Param() for query strings.
Thinking @Headers() writes response headers.
Putting filters or pagination into the request body for simple GET endpoints.

vi.title: Decorator lấy dữ liệu HTTP request
vi.displayQuestion: @Body(), @Param(), @Query() và @Headers() khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có map đúng parameter decorator của Nest với nguồn dữ liệu trong HTTP request hay không.
guidance: Dùng một URL ví dụ có path param, query param, body và header.
Nhắc tới cách lấy cả object hoặc lấy một key cụ thể.
Kết nối @Body() với DTO và validation khi phù hợp.
commonMistakes: Dùng @Param() để đọc query string.
Nghĩ @Headers() dùng để set response header.
Đưa filter hoặc pagination vào body cho endpoint GET đơn giản.

ja.title: HTTP parameter decorator
ja.displayIntent: Nest の parameter decorator を正しい HTTP request source に対応付けられるか確認する。
guidance: path param、query param、body、header を含む URL 例で説明する。
全体 object の取得と特定 key の取得を説明する。
必要に応じて @Body() を DTO と validation に結びつける。
commonMistakes: query string を @Param() で読む。
@Headers() が response header を設定すると考える。
単純な GET endpoint の filter や pagination を body に入れる。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers
sourceReferences.note: Used for built-in HTTP parameter decorators and request object mapping.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Custom decorators
sourceReferences.note: Used for built-in parameter decorator list and custom parameter decorator behavior.

intent: Assess whether the candidate knows how NestJS chooses default response status codes and when @HttpCode() should override the status for a route handler.
expectedSignals: Explains that @HttpCode(status) sets the static HTTP response status for a route handler.
Mentions Nest defaults such as 200 for most handlers and 201 for POST when returning data normally.
Gives practical examples such as @HttpCode(204) for no-content responses or @HttpCode(200) for login POST endpoints.
Clarifies that dynamic status decisions usually require throwing exceptions or using the response object carefully.
redFlags: Thinks @HttpCode() changes route path or request method.
Uses @HttpCode() for every endpoint without understanding defaults.
Returns a body with 204 No Content without noticing the semantic mismatch.
Thinks @HttpCode() still applies when fully handling the response with @Res() in library-specific mode.

scoringHints.description: Explains default statuses, static override behavior, common use cases, and interaction with normal Nest response handling.
Knows @HttpCode sets status but misses defaults or dynamic response caveats.
Recognizes it is about status codes but cannot apply it correctly.
Answer does not demonstrate HTTP semantics or Nest response behavior.

followUps.question: Why might a POST /auth/login handler use @HttpCode(200) instead of accepting Nest's default?
When would @HttpCode(204) be inappropriate even if the operation succeeded?
followUps.purpose: Check response body and HTTP semantics.

followUps.question: If you inject @Res() and call res.status(201).json(...), what does @HttpCode(200) on that method do?
followUps.purpose: Check interaction with manual response handling.

en.title: Setting route response status with @HttpCode
en.displayQuestion: What is @HttpCode() used for in NestJS?
en.displayIntent: Check HTTP status code control in normal Nest response handling.
guidance: Mention Nest's default status codes for common handlers.
Use examples like POST login returning 200 or DELETE returning 204.
Distinguish static status decorators from dynamic exception or response handling.
commonMistakes: Using @HttpCode() as if it changes routing.
Returning content with a 204 response.
Forgetting that manual @Res() handling bypasses standard Nest response decorators.

vi.title: Set response status bằng @HttpCode
vi.displayQuestion: @HttpCode() dùng để làm gì trong NestJS?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cách kiểm soát HTTP status code khi dùng response handling chuẩn của Nest.
guidance: Nhắc tới status mặc định của Nest cho các handler phổ biến.
Dùng ví dụ POST login trả 200 hoặc DELETE trả 204.
Phân biệt status tĩnh bằng decorator với xử lý động bằng exception hoặc response object.
commonMistakes: Dùng @HttpCode() như thể nó thay đổi routing.
Trả body cho response 204.
Quên rằng manual @Res() có thể bypass decorator response chuẩn của Nest.

ja.title: @HttpCode による response status 設定
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の @HttpCode() は何のために使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: Nest の標準 response handling における HTTP status code 制御を確認する。
guidance: 一般的な handler に対する Nest の default status code に触れる。
POST login が 200 を返す例や DELETE が 204 を返す例を使う。
decorator による static status と exception/response object による dynamic handling を区別する。
commonMistakes: @HttpCode() が routing を変えると考える。
204 response に body を返す。
manual @Res() が Nest の標準 response decorator を bypass し得ることを忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers status code
sourceReferences.note: Used for default response status and @HttpCode behavior.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers response headers and library-specific response
sourceReferences.note: Used for interaction with standard response handling and @Res().

intent: Check whether the candidate understands @Header() as a response header decorator and can distinguish it from @Headers(), which reads request headers.
primaryQuestion: What is @Header() and when would you use it?
expectedSignals: Explains that @Header(name, value) sets a static response header on a route handler.
Distinguishes @Header() from @Headers(), where @Headers() reads request headers.
Gives use cases such as Cache-Control, Content-Disposition, Content-Type overrides, or no-store headers.
Mentions that dynamic headers can be set through interceptors or @Res({ passthrough: true }) when needed.
redFlags: Confuses @Header() with @Headers() and says it reads incoming headers.
Uses static @Header() for values that must be calculated per request without explaining alternatives.
Ignores security or caching implications of headers such as Cache-Control.
Uses @Res() for simple static headers when @Header() would keep standard Nest response handling.

scoringHints.description: Clearly explains static response header setting, @Headers() contrast, real use cases, and dynamic alternatives.
Knows @Header sets response headers but misses dynamic-header trade-offs.
Confuses request and response header decorators or cannot provide realistic use cases.
Answer does not show practical HTTP header understanding.

followUps.question: Which decorator would read the incoming Authorization header, and which would set Cache-Control on the response?
If the header value depends on the current user, would you use @Header()? What alternative fits better?
followUps.purpose: Check static versus dynamic response-header reasoning.

followUps.question: What does Cache-Control: no-store communicate to clients or proxies?
followUps.purpose: Check HTTP semantics behind a common @Header() use case.

en.title: Setting response headers with @Header
en.displayQuestion: What is @Header() in NestJS, and when would you use it?
en.displayIntent: Check response header handling and the distinction from @Headers().
guidance: Explain that @Header() sets response headers, not request headers.
Use practical examples such as Cache-Control or Content-Disposition.
Mention dynamic alternatives when header values are request-specific.
commonMistakes: Confusing @Header() with @Headers().
Using static decorators for per-request values.
Switching to @Res() unnecessarily for simple response headers.

vi.title: Set response header bằng @Header
vi.displayQuestion: @Header() trong NestJS là gì, và khi nào bạn dùng nó?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cách set response header và phân biệt với @Headers().
guidance: Giải thích @Header() set response header, không phải đọc request header.
Dùng ví dụ thực tế như Cache-Control hoặc Content-Disposition.
Nhắc tới alternative cho header động phụ thuộc request.
commonMistakes: Nhầm @Header() với @Headers().
Dùng decorator tĩnh cho giá trị thay đổi theo từng request.
Chuyển sang @Res() không cần thiết cho response header đơn giản.

ja.title: @Header による response header 設定
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の @Header() とは何で、どのような場合に使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: response header handling と @Headers() との違いを確認する。
guidance: @Header() は request header ではなく response header を設定することを説明する。
Cache-Control や Content-Disposition などの実例を使う。
request ごとに変わる header value には別の選択肢があることを述べる。
commonMistakes: @Header() と @Headers() を混同する。
request-specific な値に static decorator を使う。
単純な response header のために不要に @Res() へ切り替える。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers response headers
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Header() behavior and examples.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers request object
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Headers() request-header extraction contrast.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands @Res() as access to the underlying platform response object and can explain the trade-off between manual response control and Nest standard response handling.
primaryQuestion: What does @Res() do? What is the tradeoff of using it vs returning data normally?
expectedSignals: Explains that @Res() injects the native response object from the underlying HTTP platform, such as Express Response or Fastify reply.
States that using @Res() without passthrough puts the handler into library-specific/manual response mode.
Mentions the developer must call res.status(), res.json(), res.send(), redirect, stream, or otherwise finish the response.
Explains trade-offs: more control for cookies, streaming, redirects, downloads, and dynamic headers, but more platform coupling and harder tests.
Mentions standard Nest features such as interceptors, response mapping, @HttpCode(), and @Header() may not work the same when the response is handled manually.
Knows @Res({ passthrough: true }) can set headers/cookies while still returning data through Nest.
redFlags: Injects @Res() in every controller by default.
Forgets to send a response and risks hanging the request.
Does not understand why interceptors or response decorators may be bypassed.
Claims @Res() is required to set any status code or header.

scoringHints.description: Covers native response object, manual mode, response completion responsibility, platform/test trade-offs, feature bypasses, and passthrough.
Understands manual response control but misses passthrough or one Nest feature interaction.
Uses @Res() mechanically without articulating when it is justified.
Cannot distinguish manual response handling from returning data normally.

followUps.question: What breaks or becomes less useful if you use @Res() and call res.json() yourself?
What happens if a handler injects @Res() but neither returns passthrough data nor calls res.send()/res.json()?
When would @Res({ passthrough: true }) be a better choice than plain @Res()?

en.title: @Res() and manual response handling
en.displayQuestion: What does @Res() do? What is the tradeoff of using it instead of returning data normally?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate whether the candidate can reason about manual response control versus Nest standard response handling.
guidance: Explain native platform response access and manual response mode.
Mention when manual control is useful: cookies, downloads, redirects, streams, or dynamic headers.
Discuss platform coupling, test cost, and Nest feature bypasses; include passthrough.
commonMistakes: Using @Res() everywhere as a habit.
Forgetting to finish the response manually.
Missing that interceptors, @HttpCode(), and @Header() depend on standard response handling.

vi.title: @Res() và manual response handling
vi.displayQuestion: @Res() làm gì? Trade-off khi dùng nó so với việc return data bình thường là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá khả năng phân tích giữa manual response control và response handling chuẩn của Nest.
guidance: Giải thích truy cập native response object và manual response mode.
Nhắc khi manual control hữu ích: cookie, download, redirect, stream hoặc dynamic header.
Phân tích platform coupling, chi phí test và việc bypass feature của Nest; nhớ nói về passthrough.
commonMistakes: Dùng @Res() ở mọi nơi theo thói quen.
Quên tự kết thúc response.
Không nhận ra interceptor, @HttpCode() và @Header() phụ thuộc vào standard response handling.

ja.title: @Res() と manual response handling
ja.displayQuestion: @Res() は何をしますか。通常どおり data を return する場合と比べた trade-off は何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: manual response control と Nest の標準 response handling の trade-off を説明できるか評価する。
guidance: native platform response への access と manual response mode を説明する。
cookie、download、redirect、stream、dynamic header など manual control が役立つ場面を挙げる。
platform coupling、test cost、Nest feature bypass、passthrough を説明する。
commonMistakes: 習慣的にすべての handler で @Res() を使う。
手動で response を完了することを忘れる。
interceptor、@HttpCode()、@Header() が standard response handling に依存する点を見落とす。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers library-specific response
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Res(), manual response mode, and passthrough behavior.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Interceptors response mapping
sourceReferences.note: Used for interceptor limitations with manual response handling.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands SetMetadata() as a low-level way to attach custom route or controller metadata that can later be read by guards, interceptors, or other framework components.
expectedSignals: Explains that SetMetadata(key, value) attaches custom metadata to a class or route handler.
Gives common examples such as roles, permissions, public routes, cache policy, rate-limit policy, or feature flags.
Mentions that SetMetadata is often wrapped in a custom decorator such as export const Roles = (...roles) => SetMetadata('roles', roles).
Connects the metadata to later reads via Reflector inside guards, interceptors, or other providers with ExecutionContext.
Understands that metadata by itself does nothing unless some component reads and enforces it.
redFlags: Thinks SetMetadata directly performs authorization or validation.
Uses raw SetMetadata everywhere instead of a named custom decorator for readability and consistency.
Cannot explain who reads the metadata later.
Stores large mutable request data in decorator metadata instead of static policy/configuration.

scoringHints.description: Explains key/value metadata, custom decorator wrapping, realistic policy examples, and enforcement via Reflector-powered guards or interceptors.
Understands custom metadata but misses the need for a reader/enforcer or clean wrapper decorator.
Knows SetMetadata syntax but cannot describe a complete use case.
Confuses metadata attachment with business logic execution.

followUps.question: How would you implement @Roles('admin') using SetMetadata()?
After metadata is set, where would you read it to enforce authorization?
followUps.purpose: Connect metadata definition to guard enforcement.

followUps.question: If no guard or interceptor reads the metadata, what effect does SetMetadata('roles', ['admin']) have?
followUps.purpose: Check that metadata alone is inert.

en.title: Custom route metadata with SetMetadata
en.displayQuestion: What is SetMetadata() used for in NestJS?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate understands custom metadata and how it becomes enforceable.
guidance: Explain key/value metadata on classes or handlers.
Use examples such as roles, permissions, public routes, cache policy, or rate limit policy.
Mention wrapping SetMetadata in custom decorators and reading it through Reflector.
commonMistakes: Thinking SetMetadata enforces authorization by itself.
Not creating a readable custom decorator around raw metadata keys.
Forgetting that another component must read the metadata.

vi.title: Custom route metadata với SetMetadata
vi.displayQuestion: SetMetadata() trong NestJS dùng để làm gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên hiểu custom metadata và cách metadata đó được enforce ra sao.
guidance: Giải thích metadata dạng key/value trên class hoặc handler.
Dùng ví dụ như roles, permissions, public route, cache policy hoặc rate-limit policy.
Nhắc việc wrap SetMetadata bằng custom decorator và đọc lại bằng Reflector.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ SetMetadata tự enforce authorization.
Không tạo custom decorator dễ đọc quanh raw metadata key.
Quên rằng phải có component khác đọc metadata.

ja.title: SetMetadata による custom route metadata
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の SetMetadata() は何のために使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: custom metadata と、それがどのように enforce されるかを理解しているか確認する。
guidance: class または handler に key/value metadata を付与することを説明する。
roles、permissions、public route、cache policy、rate limit policy などの例を使う。
SetMetadata を custom decorator で包み、Reflector で読むことを述べる。
commonMistakes: SetMetadata だけで authorization が実行されると考える。
raw metadata key のまま使い、読みやすい custom decorator を作らない。
metadata を読む component が必要なことを忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Execution context reflection and metadata
sourceReferences.note: Used for SetMetadata and Reflector usage.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Guards setting roles per handler
sourceReferences.note: Used for roles metadata examples.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands Reflector as the NestJS helper for reading custom metadata in context-aware components such as guards and interceptors, especially metadata set by SetMetadata() or Reflector.createDecorator().
primaryQuestion: What is Reflector and how does it pair with SetMetadata()?
expectedSignals: Explains that Reflector is an injectable helper from @nestjs/core used to read metadata from handlers and classes.
Connects SetMetadata('roles', roles) on a handler or controller with this.reflector.get('roles', context.getHandler()).
Mentions ExecutionContext methods getHandler() and getClass() as metadata targets.
Explains getAllAndOverride for method-overrides-controller defaults and getAllAndMerge for combining class and method metadata.
Understands the typical pattern: custom decorator sets policy metadata, guard/interceptor reads metadata, then enforces or changes behavior.
redFlags: Cannot explain the relationship between metadata keys and Reflector reads.
Reads only method metadata and accidentally ignores controller-level metadata.
Thinks Reflector creates database reflection or TypeScript compile-time types.
Uses hard-coded strings inconsistently without constants or typed decorators.

scoringHints.description: Explains Reflector injection, get/getAllAndOverride/getAllAndMerge, handler/class targets, and complete SetMetadata-to-guard flow.
Understands Reflector reads metadata but misses class-level fallback or merge/override behavior.
Can repeat Reflector.get syntax but cannot reason about metadata scopes.
Answer does not connect Reflector to custom metadata enforcement.

followUps.question: In a RolesGuard, what is the difference between context.getHandler() and context.getClass() when reading roles?
followUps.purpose: Check method versus controller metadata awareness.

followUps.question: When would you use getAllAndOverride instead of getAllAndMerge?
If @Roles('user') is on the controller and @Roles('admin') is on the method, what should your guard read if method-level roles should override defaults?

en.title: Reading metadata with Reflector
en.displayQuestion: What is Reflector, and how does it pair with SetMetadata()?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can read and enforce custom Nest metadata correctly.
guidance: Mention Reflector from @nestjs/core and injection into guards or interceptors.
Explain reading metadata from context.getHandler() and context.getClass().
Cover get, getAllAndOverride, and getAllAndMerge with a roles example.
commonMistakes: Setting metadata but never reading it.
Ignoring controller-level metadata.
Not defining consistent metadata keys or typed decorators.

vi.title: Đọc metadata bằng Reflector
vi.displayQuestion: Reflector là gì, và nó kết hợp với SetMetadata() như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có biết đọc và enforce custom metadata trong Nest đúng cách hay không.
guidance: Nhắc Reflector từ @nestjs/core và việc inject vào guard hoặc interceptor.
Giải thích đọc metadata từ context.getHandler() và context.getClass().
Bao gồm get, getAllAndOverride và getAllAndMerge bằng ví dụ roles.
commonMistakes: Set metadata nhưng không có nơi đọc lại.
Bỏ qua metadata ở controller level.
Không định nghĩa metadata key nhất quán hoặc typed decorator.

ja.title: Reflector による metadata 読み取り
ja.displayQuestion: Reflector とは何で、SetMetadata() とどのように組み合わせますか。
ja.displayIntent: Nest の custom metadata を正しく読み取り enforce できるか確認する。
guidance: @nestjs/core の Reflector と、guard/interceptor への injection に触れる。
context.getHandler() と context.getClass() から metadata を読むことを説明する。
roles 例で get、getAllAndOverride、getAllAndMerge を説明する。
commonMistakes: metadata を設定するだけで読まない。
controller-level metadata を無視する。
metadata key や typed decorator を一貫して定義しない。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Execution context reflection and metadata
sourceReferences.note: Used for Reflector, getHandler/getClass, getAllAndOverride, and getAllAndMerge.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Guards setting roles per handler
sourceReferences.note: Used for RolesGuard metadata-reading pattern.

intent: Check whether the candidate can apply multiple NestJS decorators to a route handler cleanly and understands method decorators versus parameter decorators and decorator composition.
primaryQuestion: How do you apply multiple decorators to a single route handler?
expectedSignals: Explains that multiple decorators can be stacked above the same method, for example @Post(), @HttpCode(200), @UseGuards(AuthGuard), and @UseInterceptors(...).
Distinguishes method decorators from parameter decorators inside the method signature such as @Body() dto or @Param('id') id.
Mentions that decorator order can matter for composition/evaluation and should be kept readable and conventional.
Mentions applyDecorators() as an option to bundle repeated decorator sets into a custom decorator.
redFlags: Thinks only one decorator can be used per handler.
Places parameter decorators above the method instead of on parameters.
Uses decorators to hide too much behavior without naming the abstraction clearly.
Cannot give a syntactically plausible Nest route example.

scoringHints.description: Shows a correct stacked route example, separates method and parameter decorators, and mentions applyDecorators for repeated bundles.
Understands stacked decorators but misses order/readability or composition details.
Can name decorators but cannot place them correctly in code.
Answer does not demonstrate practical Nest controller syntax.

followUps.question: Write the decorator layout for a protected POST /users route that reads a CreateUserDto and returns 201.
Which decorators go above the method, and which ones go inside the parameter list?
followUps.purpose: Check method versus parameter decorator distinction.

followUps.question: When would stacking many decorators become a readability problem, and how could you clean it up?
followUps.purpose: Lead into applyDecorators and naming abstractions.

en.title: Stacking multiple route decorators
en.displayQuestion: How do you apply multiple decorators to a single NestJS route handler?
en.displayIntent: Check practical controller syntax and decorator composition awareness.
guidance: Show stacked method decorators above the handler.
Place @Body(), @Param(), @Query(), or custom parameter decorators in the parameter list.
Mention applyDecorators() for repeated bundles such as Auth.
commonMistakes: Thinking a handler can only have one decorator.
Putting parameter decorators in the wrong position.
Creating a composed decorator with a vague name that hides important behavior.

vi.title: Stack nhiều route decorator
vi.displayQuestion: Làm sao apply nhiều decorator cho một route handler trong NestJS?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cú pháp controller thực tế và hiểu biết về decorator composition.
guidance: Minh họa nhiều method decorator được stack phía trên handler.
Đặt @Body(), @Param(), @Query() hoặc custom parameter decorator trong parameter list.
Nhắc applyDecorators() cho các bundle lặp lại như Auth.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ một handler chỉ dùng được một decorator.
Đặt parameter decorator sai vị trí.
Tạo composed decorator có tên mơ hồ và che mất hành vi quan trọng.

ja.title: 複数の route decorator の stacking
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の route handler に複数の decorator をどう適用しますか。
ja.displayIntent: 実践的な controller syntax と decorator composition の理解を確認する。
guidance: method decorator を handler の上に stack する例を示す。
@Body(), @Param(), @Query() や custom parameter decorator は parameter list に置く。
Auth などの繰り返し bundle には applyDecorators() を使えることを述べる。
commonMistakes: handler には decorator を 1 つしか使えないと考える。
parameter decorator の位置を間違える。
重要な挙動を曖昧な名前の composed decorator で隠す。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers
sourceReferences.note: Used for stacked route and parameter decorator examples.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Custom decorators decorator composition
sourceReferences.note: Used for applyDecorators composition behavior.

sourceReferences.label: TypeScript Handbook: Decorator composition
sourceReferences.note: Used for decorator stacking and composition concepts.

intent: Verify that the candidate distinguishes route path parameters from query string parameters and knows that @Get('?id') is not the correct way to define a query parameter route in NestJS.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between @Get(':id') and @Get('?id')?
expectedSignals: Explains that @Get(':id') defines a dynamic path segment such as GET /users/123 and is read with @Param('id').
Explains that query strings are not defined in the @Get() path string in Nest; they are read with @Query('id') from requests such as GET /users?id=123.
States that @Get('?id') is incorrect or at least not the intended Nest pattern for query parameters.
Connects route design semantics: path params identify a resource, query params usually filter, search, paginate, or change representation.
redFlags: Claims @Get('?id') is the normal way to handle ?id=123.
Cannot explain how @Param('id') differs from @Query('id').
Designs ambiguous routes where the same resource identity is split inconsistently between path and query.
Treats query strings as part of the route path.

scoringHints.description: Explains path parameter syntax, correct query parameter handling, why @Get('?id') is wrong, and resource-design semantics.
Distinguishes path and query params correctly but misses route design semantics.
Knows @Param and @Query names but confuses URL shapes.
Answer would likely produce incorrect Nest routes.

followUps.question: How would you implement GET /users?id=123 in a Nest controller?
For GET /users/123/orders?page=2, which parts are @Param and which are @Query?
When would you choose /users/:id versus /users?id=... for API design?

en.title: Path params versus query params
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between @Get(':id') and @Get('?id') in NestJS?
en.displayIntent: Check route parameter versus query parameter understanding.
guidance: Explain @Get(':id') with /users/123 and @Param('id').
Explain query strings with /users?id=123 and @Query('id').
State clearly that @Get('?id') is not the correct Nest pattern for query parameters.
commonMistakes: Treating query strings as part of the route path.
Using @Param('id') to read ?id=123.
Using query params for resource identity when a path parameter would be clearer.

vi.title: Path param và query param
vi.displayQuestion: @Get(':id') và @Get('?id') trong NestJS khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra hiểu biết về route parameter và query parameter.
guidance: Giải thích @Get(':id') với /users/123 và @Param('id').
Giải thích query string với /users?id=123 và @Query('id').
Nói rõ @Get('?id') không phải pattern đúng của Nest để xử lý query parameter.
commonMistakes: Xem query string là một phần của route path.
Dùng @Param('id') để đọc ?id=123.
Dùng query param cho resource identity trong khi path parameter rõ nghĩa hơn.

ja.title: path param と query param
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の @Get(':id') と @Get('?id') は何が違いますか。
ja.displayIntent: route parameter と query parameter の理解を確認する。
guidance: /users/123 と @Param('id') を使って @Get(':id') を説明する。
/users?id=123 と @Query('id') を使って query string を説明する。
@Get('?id') は query parameter を扱う Nest の正しい pattern ではないと明確に述べる。
commonMistakes: query string を route path の一部として扱う。
?id=123 を @Param('id') で読もうとする。
path parameter の方が明確な resource identity に query param を使う。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers route parameters
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Get(':id') and @Param() route parameter behavior.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers query parameters
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Query() query string extraction.

intent: Assess whether the candidate understands the different responsibilities and request lifecycle positions of @UseGuards(), @UseInterceptors(), and @UsePipes().
expectedSignals: Explains @UseGuards() binds guards that decide whether a request can proceed, commonly for authentication, authorization, and policy checks.
Explains @UsePipes() binds pipes that transform and validate incoming arguments before the handler runs.
Explains @UseInterceptors() binds interceptors that can run before/after the handler, transform responses, map errors, cache, log, or wrap streams.
Mentions they can be applied at method, controller, or global scope.
Understands lifecycle order at a high level: guards decide access before handler, pipes prepare parameters, interceptors wrap handler execution and response flow.
redFlags: Uses guards for DTO validation or pipes for authorization.
Cannot explain what each component is responsible for.
Thinks interceptors are only middleware and cannot affect responses.
Does not know where to bind these components or assumes they only work globally.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates guard, pipe, and interceptor responsibilities, binding scopes, and lifecycle position with realistic examples.
Gets responsibilities mostly right but misses lifecycle order or one advanced interceptor capability.
Can name the decorators but mixes responsibilities.
Answer would lead to incorrect Nest request-pipeline design.

followUps.question: Where would you put role checking, DTO validation, and response envelope formatting: guard, pipe, or interceptor?
Which of these can prevent the route handler from running before it executes?
followUps.purpose: Check guard and interceptor short-circuit awareness.

followUps.question: Why is a ValidationPipe a bad place to check whether the user is an admin?

en.title: Guards, interceptors, and pipes
en.displayQuestion: What do @UseGuards(), @UseInterceptors(), and @UsePipes() do in NestJS?
en.displayIntent: Check request lifecycle responsibilities for guards, interceptors, and pipes.
guidance: Map each decorator to its component type and responsibility.
Use examples: auth guard, ValidationPipe, response-transform interceptor.
Mention method, controller, and global binding scopes.
commonMistakes: Putting authorization logic in pipes.
Using interceptors when simple validation is needed.
Forgetting that interceptors can transform or wrap responses.

vi.title: Guards, interceptors và pipes
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra trách nhiệm của guard, interceptor và pipe trong request lifecycle.
guidance: Map từng decorator với component type và trách nhiệm tương ứng.
Dùng ví dụ: auth guard, ValidationPipe, response-transform interceptor.
Nhắc tới scope method, controller và global.
commonMistakes: Đưa authorization logic vào pipe.
Dùng interceptor khi chỉ cần validation đơn giản.
Quên interceptor có thể transform hoặc wrap response.

ja.title: guards、interceptors、pipes
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の @UseGuards(), @UseInterceptors(), @UsePipes() は何をしますか。
ja.displayIntent: request lifecycle における guard、interceptor、pipe の責務を確認する。
guidance: 各 decorator を component type と責務に対応付ける。
auth guard、ValidationPipe、response-transform interceptor などの例を使う。
method、controller、global の binding scope に触れる。
commonMistakes: authorization logic を pipe に入れる。
単純な validation に interceptor を使う。
interceptor が response を transform/wrap できることを忘れる。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Guards
sourceReferences.note: Used for @UseGuards() and guard binding scopes.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Interceptors
sourceReferences.note: Used for @UseInterceptors(), response mapping, and binding scopes.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Pipes
sourceReferences.note: Used for @UsePipes(), validation, transformation, and binding scopes.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can reason about method-level, controller-level, and global binding of NestJS decorators and pipeline components, including scope, precedence, and dependency-injection implications.
primaryQuestion: What is the difference between applying a decorator at method level vs controller level vs globally?
expectedSignals: Explains method-level decorators affect only one handler and are best for route-specific behavior.
Explains controller-level decorators affect all handlers in that controller and are useful for shared policy, prefix-level concerns, or defaults.
Explains global binding affects all routes in the application and is useful for cross-cutting concerns such as validation, logging, auth defaults, or response shaping.
Mentions trade-offs: global consistency versus accidental broad impact; method precision versus repetition.
Mentions DI nuance for global guards/interceptors/pipes registered with app.useGlobal* versus APP_GUARD, APP_INTERCEPTOR, or APP_PIPE providers.
Understands metadata precedence or combination may require Reflector getAllAndOverride/getAllAndMerge.
redFlags: Thinks controller-level and global-level are the same.
Applies security-sensitive behavior globally without thinking about public routes or overrides.
Copies the same decorator to every method when controller-level or composed decorators would be cleaner.
Does not know that global components registered outside a module can have dependency injection limitations.

scoringHints.description: Explains scope differences, examples, repetition versus blast-radius trade-offs, DI implications, and metadata precedence/merge behavior.
Gets method/controller/global scope right but misses DI or precedence details.
Understands decorators can be applied in different places but cannot reason about impact.
Answer would lead to uncontrolled or repetitive pipeline configuration.

followUps.question: Why might a global AuthGuard be useful, and what problem does it create for public endpoints?
followUps.purpose: Check broad policy and override reasoning.

followUps.question: If every route in UsersController requires the same guard, where would you bind it and why?
What is the difference between app.useGlobalGuards(new RolesGuard()) and registering { provide: APP_GUARD, useClass: RolesGuard }?
followUps.purpose: Check dependency-injection implication of global registration.

en.title: Method, controller, and global decorator scope
en.displayQuestion: What is the difference between applying a decorator at method level, controller level, and globally?
en.displayIntent: Evaluate scope, precedence, and blast-radius reasoning for Nest decorators and pipeline components.
guidance: Compare method-level precision, controller-level defaults, and global cross-cutting behavior.
Discuss repetition versus broad impact using guards, pipes, or interceptors.
Mention DI implications for global registration and metadata override/merge when relevant.
commonMistakes: Applying global behavior without planning public-route exceptions.
Repeating the same decorator on every method.
Ignoring dependency injection limitations of app.useGlobal* registration.

vi.title: Scope method, controller và global của decorator
vi.displayQuestion: Decorator apply ở method level, controller level và global khác nhau như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Đánh giá khả năng phân tích scope, precedence và blast radius của decorator và pipeline component trong Nest.
guidance: So sánh method-level chính xác, controller-level làm default, và global cho cross-cutting behavior.
Phân tích repetition so với ảnh hưởng quá rộng bằng ví dụ guard, pipe hoặc interceptor.
Nhắc tới DI khi đăng ký global và override/merge metadata khi liên quan.
commonMistakes: Apply behavior global mà không tính public-route exception.
Lặp cùng một decorator trên mọi method.
Bỏ qua giới hạn dependency injection của app.useGlobal* registration.

ja.title: method、controller、global の decorator scope
ja.displayQuestion: decorator を method level、controller level、global に適用する違いは何ですか。
ja.displayIntent: Nest decorator と pipeline component の scope、precedence、blast radius を評価する。
guidance: method-level の精密さ、controller-level の default、global の cross-cutting behavior を比較する。
guard、pipe、interceptor を例に repetition と broad impact の trade-off を説明する。
global registration の DI implication と metadata override/merge に触れる。
commonMistakes: public-route exception を考えずに global behavior を適用する。
同じ decorator をすべての method に繰り返す。
app.useGlobal* registration の dependency injection 制限を無視する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Guards binding guards
sourceReferences.note: Used for method, controller, and global guard scope plus APP_GUARD pattern.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Interceptors binding interceptors
sourceReferences.note: Used for interceptor scope and APP_INTERCEPTOR registration.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Execution context reflection and metadata
sourceReferences.note: Used for method/class metadata override and merge behavior.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands @Req() as access to the underlying request object and can explain when it is appropriate versus using more specific parameter decorators.
primaryQuestion: What is @Req() and when would you use it?
expectedSignals: Explains that @Req() injects the underlying request object, such as Express Request or Fastify request.
Mentions it exposes request data like params, query, body, headers, IP, cookies, user, raw body, and framework-specific properties.
Explains that specific decorators like @Body(), @Param(), @Query(), and @Headers() are preferable for simple extraction.
Gives appropriate use cases such as reading req.user after an auth guard, accessing cookies/rawBody/platform-specific fields, or passing request context to lower-level logic carefully.
Mentions trade-offs such as platform coupling, harder tests, and less explicit handler signatures if overused.
redFlags: Uses @Req() as the default for all input extraction.
Does not know that @Body(), @Param(), @Query(), and @Headers() avoid manual request parsing.
Couples business services directly to Express Request without need.
Confuses @Req() with @Res() and response handling.

scoringHints.description: Explains native request access, appropriate use cases, preference for specific parameter decorators, and platform/test trade-offs.
Knows @Req() exposes the request but misses overuse trade-offs.
Uses @Req() for everything and cannot explain cleaner alternatives.
Cannot distinguish request access from response control.

followUps.question: If an AuthGuard attaches req.user, how would you read that user in a controller without manually parsing headers?
Why is @Body('email') often cleaner than @Req() request.body.email?
followUps.purpose: Check explicit parameter extraction and testability reasoning.

followUps.question: Should a domain service accept the whole Express Request object? What risk does that create?

en.title: Accessing the request with @Req
en.displayQuestion: What is @Req() in NestJS, and when would you use it?
en.displayIntent: Check request object access and the trade-off against specific parameter decorators.
guidance: Explain that @Req() injects the native request object.
Prefer @Body(), @Param(), @Query(), and @Headers() for simple data extraction.
Use examples such as req.user, cookies, raw body, or platform-specific fields.
commonMistakes: Using @Req() for every controller input.
Passing framework request objects deep into domain services.
Confusing @Req() with @Res().

vi.title: Truy cập request bằng @Req
vi.displayQuestion: @Req() trong NestJS là gì, và khi nào bạn dùng nó?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra cách truy cập request object và trade-off so với parameter decorator cụ thể.
guidance: Giải thích @Req() inject native request object.
Ưu tiên @Body(), @Param(), @Query() và @Headers() cho việc lấy dữ liệu đơn giản.
Dùng ví dụ như req.user, cookie, raw body hoặc field phụ thuộc platform.
commonMistakes: Dùng @Req() cho mọi input trong controller.
Truyền framework request object sâu vào domain service.
Nhầm @Req() với @Res().

ja.title: @Req による request access
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の @Req() とは何で、どのような場合に使いますか。
ja.displayIntent: request object access と具体的な parameter decorator との trade-off を確認する。
guidance: @Req() が native request object を inject することを説明する。
単純な data extraction には @Body(), @Param(), @Query(), @Headers() を優先する。
req.user、cookie、raw body、platform-specific field などの例を使う。
commonMistakes: controller input のすべてに @Req() を使う。
framework request object を domain service に深く渡す。
@Req() と @Res() を混同する。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Controllers request object
sourceReferences.note: Used for @Req() and built-in request parameter decorators.

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Raw body
sourceReferences.note: Used for raw request body use case with @Req().

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can create a simple custom NestJS parameter decorator using createParamDecorator and understands data arguments, ExecutionContext, and pipe interaction.
primaryQuestion: How do you create a simple custom parameter decorator using createParamDecorator?
expectedSignals: Imports createParamDecorator and ExecutionContext from @nestjs/common.
Defines a decorator factory such as CurrentUser = createParamDecorator((data, ctx) => { const req = ctx.switchToHttp().getRequest(); return data ? req.user?.[data] : req.user; }).
Explains that the data argument is the value passed to the decorator, for example @CurrentUser('id').
Explains ExecutionContext lets the decorator access the HTTP request through switchToHttp(), and can also support other transport contexts if designed for them.
Mentions custom parameter decorators behave like built-in ones and can work with pipes; ValidationPipe needs validateCustomDecorators for validation of custom-decorated args.
redFlags: Creates a method decorator instead of a parameter decorator.
Reads global state instead of using ExecutionContext.
Returns the entire request object when only a user or one field is needed.
Does not handle missing req.user safely.
Assumes pipes always validate custom-decorated parameters without configuration.

scoringHints.description: Provides correct createParamDecorator code, explains data and ExecutionContext, handles optional fields safely, and mentions pipe interaction.
Can write the decorator and use ctx.switchToHttp(), but misses typing, missing-user handling, or pipes.
Understands the idea but code would not work as a parameter decorator.
Cannot describe custom parameter decorator mechanics.

followUps.question: Write a @CurrentUser('id') decorator that returns req.user.id when available.
Where does req.user usually come from before your custom decorator reads it?
Why might @CurrentUser() be cleaner than injecting @Req() in every handler?
Will ValidationPipe validate custom parameter decorators by default? What option matters?

en.title: Custom parameter decorators
en.displayQuestion: How do you create a simple custom parameter decorator using createParamDecorator?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can implement and use a Nest custom parameter decorator.
guidance: Use createParamDecorator with data and ExecutionContext.
Show a CurrentUser example that reads request.user or a selected field.
Mention safe missing-user handling and interaction with pipes.
commonMistakes: Building a method decorator instead of a parameter decorator.
Using @Req() everywhere instead of encapsulating repeated request extraction.
Assuming custom-decorated parameters are always validated by ValidationPipe by default.

vi.title: Custom parameter decorator
vi.displayQuestion: Làm sao tạo một custom parameter decorator đơn giản bằng createParamDecorator?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có thể implement và sử dụng custom parameter decorator trong Nest hay không.
guidance: Dùng createParamDecorator với data và ExecutionContext.
Minh họa CurrentUser đọc request.user hoặc một field được chọn.
Nhắc cách xử lý khi user thiếu và tương tác với pipe.
commonMistakes: Tạo method decorator thay vì parameter decorator.
Dùng @Req() ở mọi nơi thay vì đóng gói logic lấy request lặp lại.
Cho rằng parameter dùng custom decorator luôn được ValidationPipe validate mặc định.

ja.title: custom parameter decorator
ja.displayQuestion: createParamDecorator を使って simple custom parameter decorator をどう作りますか。
ja.displayIntent: Nest の custom parameter decorator を実装して使えるか確認する。
guidance: data と ExecutionContext を受け取る createParamDecorator を使う。
request.user または選択された field を読む CurrentUser の例を示す。
user が無い場合の安全な扱いと pipe との関係に触れる。
commonMistakes: parameter decorator ではなく method decorator を作る。
繰り返しの request extraction を encapsulate せず、あらゆる場所で @Req() を使う。
custom decorator の parameter が ValidationPipe により常に default で validation されると思い込む。

sourceReferences.label: NestJS docs: Execution context
sourceReferences.note: Used for ExecutionContext and switchToHttp context access.

intent: Check whether the candidate understands applyDecorators() as a Nest helper for composing multiple decorators into a reusable higher-level decorator, and can identify when composition improves readability versus hides behavior.
primaryQuestion: What is applyDecorators() and when is it useful?
expectedSignals: Explains that applyDecorators() composes multiple decorators and returns one decorator that applies all of them.
Gives a practical example such as @Auth('admin') combining SetMetadata or @Roles, @UseGuards, @ApiBearerAuth, and Swagger response decorators.
Mentions usefulness for repeated route policies, consistent auth bundles, documentation bundles, cache policies, or versioning metadata.
Explains the trade-off: it improves consistency and reduces repetition but can hide behavior if the composed decorator name is vague or too broad.
Mentions that not every third-party decorator is safely composable and Swagger has known exceptions such as @ApiHideProperty().
redFlags: Thinks applyDecorators() is required whenever multiple decorators are used.
Uses a composed decorator with unclear behavior that makes security or response semantics hard to inspect.
Cannot show what decorators would be bundled in a real example.
Assumes all decorators from all libraries compose safely.

scoringHints.description: Explains decorator composition, gives a realistic Auth-style example, discusses readability/security trade-offs, and notes composability caveats.
Understands bundling repeated decorators but misses caveats or naming trade-offs.
Knows the helper name but cannot explain why or when to use it.
Answer does not demonstrate practical decorator composition knowledge.

followUps.question: Design an @AdminOnly() decorator using applyDecorators(). Which decorators would you include?
How can a composed decorator make security harder to review?
followUps.purpose: Check abstraction and readability trade-off awareness.

followUps.question: Can you always wrap any third-party decorator with applyDecorators()? What caveat should you know?

en.title: Decorator composition with applyDecorators
en.displayQuestion: What is applyDecorators(), and when is it useful in NestJS?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can compose decorators into a clear reusable abstraction.
guidance: Explain that applyDecorators() bundles multiple decorators into one custom decorator.
Use an Auth or AdminOnly example with metadata, guards, and API documentation decorators.
Discuss repetition reduction, consistent policy, readability, and composability caveats.
commonMistakes: Using applyDecorators() when simple stacking is clearer.
Hiding important security behavior behind a vague decorator name.
Assuming every library decorator composes safely.

vi.title: Compose decorator bằng applyDecorators
vi.displayQuestion: applyDecorators() là gì, và khi nào nó hữu ích trong NestJS?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ứng viên có biết compose nhiều decorator thành abstraction tái sử dụng rõ ràng hay không.
guidance: Giải thích applyDecorators() gom nhiều decorator thành một custom decorator.
Dùng ví dụ Auth hoặc AdminOnly gồm metadata, guard và decorator tài liệu API.
Phân tích giảm lặp, policy nhất quán, readability và caveat về khả năng compose.
commonMistakes: Dùng applyDecorators() khi stack decorator trực tiếp còn rõ hơn.
Che hành vi security quan trọng sau một tên decorator mơ hồ.
Cho rằng decorator của mọi thư viện đều compose an toàn.

ja.title: applyDecorators による decorator composition
ja.displayQuestion: NestJS の applyDecorators() とは何で、どのような場合に有用ですか。
ja.displayIntent: 複数の decorator を明確な reusable abstraction に compose できるか確認する。
guidance: applyDecorators() が複数の decorator を 1 つの custom decorator にまとめることを説明する。
metadata、guard、API documentation decorator を含む Auth または AdminOnly の例を使う。
重複削減、一貫した policy、readability、composability caveat を説明する。
commonMistakes: 直接 stack した方が明確な場面で applyDecorators() を使う。
重要な security behavior を曖昧な decorator 名の背後に隠す。
すべての library decorator が安全に compose できると思い込む。
